Land Rover Range Rover P530 Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Range Rover P530 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S68 engine typically costs £10,000–£16,000 supply only. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500. Main dealer new engines can exceed £35,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Range Rover P530 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S68 is BMW's most refined hot-vee V8 yet — a genuine step forward from the N63. It's rated ★★★★☆, but hot-vee architecture brings heat-related maintenance challenges: oil leaks from gaskets, cooling system vulnerability, and PCV valve issues. No catastrophic bottom-end design flaws have emerged.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Range Rover P530?

acceptedAnswer.text: Hot-vee oil leaks (valve cover gaskets, turbo oil lines, oil cooler seals), cooling system failures (plastic crossover pipes, water pump), and PCV valve/oil separator issues are the most common. All are repairable if caught early.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover P530 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Against a £80,000–£120,000+ Range Rover P530, a £10,000–£16,000 recon engine is a manageable 10–15% of the vehicle's value — well worth it. Given the L460's strong retained values, replacement is almost always viable. Scrap only if the vehicle has suffered catastrophic damage well beyond the engine.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Range Rover's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: For the P530, the engine code is S68 — it's on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The P530 badge signifies the 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8. If you're unsure, tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the S68's hot-vee oil leaks, or does it need a new engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — hot-vee oil leaks are repairable. Valve cover gaskets, turbo oil lines, and oil cooler seals can all be replaced without removing the engine. Early detection is key — oil leaks can damage adjacent electrical components if ignored.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover P530 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Straightforward gasket replacement; catch it before electrical damage
Replace plastic pipes as a preventative measure at 50,000 miles
Straightforward replacement; don't let oil consumption worsen
Replace as a preventative item at 60,000 miles
Not a common S68 failure — if it occurs, replacement is the only option
Repairable by specialists; do not drive with boost issues

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Range Rover P530? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Land Rover service history with documented oil-change intervals
* Evidence of preventative cooling system maintenance (crossover pipes, water pump)
* Hot-vee oil cooler gaskets replaced as a preventative measure
* Post-2023 build with any early S68 software updates applied
avoidIf: * Service history shows any evidence of oil leaks or coolant top-ups with no diagnosis
* Burning oil smell from the engine bay — potential hot-vee gasket failure
* Low coolant level with no visible leak — potential crossover pipe failure
* Engine warning light present with no diagnosis documentation

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The S68 is BMW's most refined hot-vee V8 yet — a genuine step forward from the N63 "N63 nightmare" reputation. But hot-vee architecture still brings heat-related maintenance challenges: oil leaks from gaskets and seals, and cooling system vulnerability. Buy on service history and proof of preventative cooling system work. If you're replacing, a reconditioned unit with a 12-month warranty is the sweet spot — dealer new engines can exceed £35,000.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover P530 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle or tick; the S68 should start smoothly and quietly
* Service History — Including which engine era (S68 is the only P530 engine — verify this before purchase)
* Oil Leaks — Check the valley between cylinder banks for oil pooling (hot-vee gasket failure)
* Coolant Level — Is the coolant low with no visible leak? Crossover pipe risk
* Burning Oil Smell — Any smell from the engine bay after a drive? Hot-vee oil leak indicator
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 3,000 miles? If so, investigate PCV/oil separator
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved cooling or fuelling issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures; the S68 should pass easily if well-maintained
